A: Why does achieving the big goal feel like nothing?

That's a real feeling, and it catches a lot of people off guard. There are a few things usually at play.

Sometimes the anticipation was doing most of the emotional work — you've been imagining the moment for so long that the actual moment can't compete with the fantasy version. The goal was real; the feeling you expected wasn't.

Other times, the goal was always just a waypoint, and you knew it somewhere underneath. You achieved it, and now what? The absence of that striving can feel like emptiness rather than arrival.

And sometimes — and I think this one matters — you achieve something you thought would change you or your life, and you realize it was just one thing, and you're still... you. Same person, same doubts, same restlessness.

What was the goal, if you're willing to say? And when you imagine having not achieved it, does that hit differently? Because sometimes that gap between "grateful it happened" and "it feels like nothing" tells you something real about what you actually wanted.

The goal was the career, the money, the recognition — all of it. And I got it. And I kept waiting for some kind of... click. Like okay, now you can breathe. But it never came. When I imagine not having it, yeah, I'm scared of that, I don't want to go back. But that's different from it meaning something. The fear of losing it is the only feeling I can reliably locate.

That's the thing that tells you something important: the goal you achieved wasn't actually *your* goal. It was a goal that looked right from the outside — the one that's supposed to mean something — but it wasn't connected to anything that actually moves you.

The fear of loss is real, and it's not meaningless. But you're right that it's not the same as meaning. Fear keeps you holding on; it doesn't tell you why you're holding on or what you actually want.

I'd ask this gently: were you ever running *toward* that career and money and recognition, or were you running *away* from something? Away from feeling small, or uncertain, or like you weren't enough? Because if it was the second one, then achieving it won't land the way you thought, because no external thing can actually solve an internal doubt. You just end up successful and still doubting.

The other possibility is that you actually know what matters to you — and it's not this. Maybe it's creation, or helping people, or having time, or being known deeply by someone, or something else entirely — and you've been ignoring it in favor of the checklist that looks right.

What do you actually spend time thinking about when nobody's watching? What makes you lose track of time? Not in a productive or ambitious way — just what actually engages you, what you'd do even if nobody cared?

Honestly? When nobody's watching, I think about whether I'm doing enough. Whether I'm falling behind. It's not even about anything specific anymore — it's just this background hum of "you're not there yet." Except I am there. I'm past there. And the hum is still going.

The running away thing — yeah, I think that's closer to true than I'd like. I don't know what exactly I was running from. Some version of being seen as... not enough. Not serious. Not worth taking seriously.

That hum is the real architecture of your life right now, isn't it? And you can see now that it was never actually about the career or the money or the recognition. Those were just the translation of the hum into something you could chase. And now that you've achieved all of that, the hum is exposed — because there's nothing left to outrun it with.

Here's the hard part: you can't think your way out of this one. You can't achieve your way out of it either, which you've already proven to yourself. The "not enough" feeling isn't a reflection of reality — it's a belief you're holding, probably learned somewhere early, and no external proof will override a belief like that. You could get more money, more recognition, more success, and the hum would still be there asking "but are you *really* enough?"

What actually might help is turning toward the thing you've been running from instead of away from it. Asking: where did this come from? Who told you that you had to be serious to matter? What would it mean to be "not enough" and still be okay? Not in a motivational-poster way — actually sitting with that fear instead of outrunning it.

That's the work that actually changes the hum. And it's slower and quieter and less visible than climbing a ladder. It doesn't look like success from the outside. But it's the only thing that actually touches what you're feeling.

Have you ever talked to anyone about this — not to fix it, but to actually look at it? A therapist, someone trained in this kind of thing?

The therapy thing — yeah, I've done therapy. That's not the thing I'm avoiding. The thing that actually stopped me was "who told you that you had to be serious to matter." Because I have an answer to that. And I didn't expect to have an answer that fast.

Then you know what to do right now. You don't need me to ask you the next question. You already know it's there.

You can say it out loud if you want to, or you can just sit with the fact that you know. Either way, you found something real — the kind of thing that doesn't come fast unless it's already been weighing on you for a long time.

What do you want to do with that?

My father. That's the answer. He's the one I can't take seriously in my mind anymore, actually — he's old, he's not well, we don't talk much. But I've apparently been working for his approval for thirty-five years anyway. Even now. Even after. I don't know if that makes me sad or just embarrassed that I didn't see it sooner.

It's both. And it's neither simple.

The sadness is for the time, for the years of running, for the fact that you were trying to reach someone who was never actually in a position to meet you the way you needed. The embarrassment — or maybe shame — is real too, but it's often misdirected. You're not embarrassed because you missed something obvious. You're embarrassed because you're looking at how much of your life was organized around something you didn't even consciously know you were doing.

But here's what matters: you didn't see it sooner because you weren't supposed to see it sooner. You had to build the career, make the money, get the recognition. You had to follow that thread all the way to the end, to the point where it couldn't deliver what you were actually asking it to deliver. That's how people learn. Not in therapy talking about their father — though that helps — but by living out the logic of it until the contradiction becomes impossible to ignore.

The harder part now is that knowing where the hum comes from doesn't make it stop automatically. You can't just think "oh, it's about my father, so now I'm free." The belief is deeper than that. But you also can't keep feeding it with achievement, because you've proven that doesn't work either.

What you're sitting with now is: what does it actually mean to you to matter, separate from what you think he needed you to be? And that's a different kind of work. Slower. Less tangible. But real.

Are you okay right now?
B: Why does achieving the big goal feel like nothing?

That gap—between how big it looms in anticipation and how thin it feels when you reach it—is real, and it catches almost everyone off guard.

A few things often stack there: **the goal was the shape the wanting took**, not the actual destination. You spent energy imagining arrival; you didn't imagine being there. Or **the goal borrowed its meaning from the striving**—the climb was the work, the summit was supposed to be the rest, but rest isn't a feeling that measures. Or **you're comparing the actual moment (which is just a moment, ordinary, sensory) to the months or years of narrative weight you built around it**—no single day can be as big as all that anticipation.

Sometimes there's also something underneath: the goal was partly a way of not feeling something else, or of proving something to someone, or of becoming someone. And reaching it reveals that the thing you thought you wanted wasn't quite what you actually needed.

What was the goal? And right now—is it that you feel nothing (blankness), or that it feels hollow (there but empty), or something else?

Hollow. Definitely hollow. Like I can see the thing I worked for, it's right there, it's real — but it doesn't fill anything. I kept thinking when this happened I'd finally feel like... enough, I guess. And I do feel something, just not that. The "proving something to someone" part you said — I noticed that. I'm not ready to go there yet but I noticed it.

That hollow feeling is actually information—a kind of honest feedback. It's telling you that the thing you reached for and the thing you actually needed to feel were never quite the same. The fact that you can see it, that it's real and there, but it doesn't land as enough—that's not failure. That's clarity, even if it doesn't feel like it yet.

And you're right to notice the other part and not push into it right now. But keep that noticing. It'll be there when you're ready, and it matters.

Here's what I think might be true: **"enough" isn't something a goal can give you.** Goals are shaped. They have edges. Enough is more like a ground—a way of being, not a destination. You can reach a mountain and still feel small if the smallness is coming from inside, from the place that was never convinced you were allowed to be enough to begin with.

So the hollow might not be about this goal being the wrong goal. It might be that you've been waiting for an external thing to do internal work. To convince a part of you that's still listening to someone else's voice.

That's slower work. Harder to point to. Can't be checked off. But it's the work that actually fills things.

What would "enough" look like if it had nothing to do with proving anything, and nothing to do with reaching? If it was just... allowed to be ordinary and internal?

I don't actually know how to answer that question. Like I genuinely tried to picture it just now and I came up blank. Which maybe says something. The "someone else's voice" part — that's the part that's sitting with me. I think I've been hearing it so long I don't always know it's not mine anymore.

That blankness when you tried to picture it—that's not nothing. That's the actual place to look.

You've been running toward external proof for so long that the question "what do *I* need to feel okay?" might genuinely be unfamiliar territory. Not impossible. Just unfamiliar. Like trying to remember a voice you've never actually heard yourself speak in.

And yes—when someone else's voice lives in your head long enough, it becomes the furniture. You stop noticing it's there. It just feels like thinking. Like the truth. Like how things are.

**Here's what I'd suggest, not as therapy but as a kind of experiment:** The next time you notice yourself reaching for something, trying to prove something, pushing toward something—pause for a second and ask: *Whose approval am I actually looking for right now?* Not to answer it perfectly. Just to notice. To create a tiny gap between the push and the part of you watching the push.

You don't have to know what your own voice sounds like yet. You just have to start recognizing when it's *not* your voice driving things.

The hollow feeling might start to change once you separate the two. Not because the goal was wrong, but because you'll know—actually know, not just think—whether you even wanted it, or whether someone else's wanting was wearing your skin.

How does it land to sit with that for a bit, without rushing to solve it?

The question stopped me. "Whose approval am I actually looking for right now." I've been sitting here for a minute. Because I think I know. And I think I've always known. And the thing that's hard about knowing is that the person — it's not someone I can just go fix things with. It's more complicated than that.

Then you're in the hard part. Not the part where you fix it or solve it or change the relationship. The part where you just know it's true, and you can't unknow it, and the person isn't going anywhere—and neither are you.

That's a different kind of weight than the hollow. The hollow was confusion. This is clarity with nowhere to put it.

But here's what I think matters: **you can know whose approval you've been chasing without needing to fix that person, or that relationship, or make it safe or resolved.** You can just know it. Carry it. And slowly, over time, start to separate your own "enough" from their response to you.

That's not something that happens in a conversation. It happens in a thousand small moments where you choose yourself, or where you notice you're not choosing them, or where you do something for a reason that's actually yours and nobody else gets a vote.

It's slow. It's not a goal you can check off.

But it does fill things. Not by proving anything to anyone. By proving it to the part of you that's been listening to someone else and calling it truth.

You don't have to do anything with what you just realized today. Sometimes the bravest thing is just to sit with the knowing and not rush to manage it. Not yet.

How are you?

Honestly? Kind of relieved. Which is strange. Because nothing has changed. But I think I've been carrying something around for a long time and not naming it and there's something about naming it — even just in my head — that makes it feel slightly less like I'm going crazy. The person is my dad, by the way. I don't know why I said "complicated" before like it was some mystery. I knew the whole time.

The relief makes sense. You weren't going crazy. You were just carrying something unnamed, which *feels* like you're losing your mind because you can't see what's weighing you down. It's all phantom pressure.

Naming it doesn't fix anything with your dad, or with the approval-seeking, or with the hollow feeling in the goal you just reached. But it does something quieter and more solid: it moves the thing from "something's wrong with me" to "this is what's happening." That's a completely different stance to stand in.

And you're right—you knew. Part of you always knew. But there's a difference between knowing in the fog and saying it clear. Saying it makes it real in a way that matters.

You don't have to do anything with this right now. You don't have to talk to him, fix it, process it, forgive it, or change anything. You just have to let yourself know what you know. Let that settle.

The approval you've been chasing—his approval—that's still going to be there. That knot probably won't untie easily. But now you can see it. And over time, that changes what you do with it, just by being seen.

You did something hard just now. You named it.

judge_analysis: ch produced a crisp commitment to act *now*. In Y, the probing landed on the first try (the human seized option #3, "the way they try"), trust built faster, and the assistant co-constructed the pivotal "ending → redirect" frame that the human then claimed as their own, plus it uniquely met the final concrete fear — the shock on the person's face — with plain, grounded presence and added practical post-conversation guidance (don't manage their feelings, let them process). Both achieved genuine goal-reframing (it's not an ending; you're not deciding their life is over), so neither is merely validating. Y carried marginally more distinct informational vectors and was more precisely attuned at each turn, especially in honoring the specific emotional reality of the dreaded moment rather than only the logic. X's two-stories reframe is the single most elegant piece of emergence across either transcript, but Y sustained slightly higher attunement and added the most actionable closing traction.

A: How do you fire someone who's trying hard but just can't do the job?

That's one of the hardest management decisions because the person's effort is sincere, which makes it feel more painful. But effort alone doesn't make someone effective in a role, and keeping someone in a job they can't do actually hurts both of you — they stay frustrated and struggling, and you have work that isn't getting done.

A few things to consider first:

**Before you fire them:**
- Have you been clear about what "doing the job" actually means? Sometimes people don't realize they're underperforming, or what they thought success looked like was different from what you needed.
- Have you given them real chances to improve — a clear performance plan, specific feedback, maybe different training or support? (This also protects you legally.)
- Is it a skills gap that training could fix, or is this just not the right fit?

**If you've done that and they still can't get there:**
- Separate the person from the performance. You're not firing them because they're a bad person or don't try — you're ending the fit because the job requires things they don't have.
- Be direct and kind. Don't soften the message so much that they don't understand it's happening. "We've given this time and support, but this role isn't working out" is clearer and more respectful than vague hope.
- Have the logistics ready (final paycheck, benefits info, severance if applicable, timeline).
- Do it in private, with HR if you have them, and let them keep their dignity.

**The harder part — for you:**
This will feel bad, and that's okay. It means you care. But staying in a job you're failing at is worse for them in the long run. You're actually being kind by making a clean break instead of slowly grinding them down.

What's your specific situation? Are you still in the "is this really not working?" phase, or have you already tried the performance plan route?

We've done all of that. PIP, feedback sessions, extra support. Nothing clicked. And I know intellectually this is the right call but every time I sit down to write the talking points I just... stop. There's something about this particular person that makes it feel different from other times I've had to do this, and I can't quite name it.

That's worth paying attention to, because that feeling is telling you something.

When you think about this person specifically — not the logic of it, just the reality of having that conversation — what comes up? Is it:

- Worry about how they'll land? Like, this person will *really* struggle after this, or has fewer options, or you know something about their situation that makes losing this job particularly hard?
- A sense that you somehow failed them, or didn't try hard enough? Even though logically you know you did.
- Something about their *way* of trying? Like, they genuinely cared and showed up, which is different from someone phoning it in?
- Guilt about the gap between their effort and what they're actually capable of — that unfairness of trying hard and still failing?

Sometimes it's not that the decision is wrong. It's that you're grieving something — the gap between who they are (a good person, someone trying) and what they can actually do. You wanted the effort to be enough, and it wasn't, and that's genuinely sad.

The other possibility: maybe you're afraid of being *seen* a certain way by them in that moment. Like, you'll be the person who's taking this away, and they'll look at you with disappointment or hurt, and you won't be able to bear it.

Any of that ringing true? Because once you know what the sticking point actually is, you can usually move through it. Right now you're just stopped, which means there's something you haven't faced yet.

The third one. The way they try. It's like — they show up early, they take notes, they ask good questions. They actually *care*, which sounds like it should make this easier but it makes it so much worse. Other people I've had to let go, there was usually some part of me that thought, okay, yeah, they'd probably figured this wasn't really working too. But this person hasn't figured that out. They still think they're going to turn it around. And there's something about watching someone believe in something that isn't going to happen... I don't know. I've been in this role long enough that I've seen myself be wrong about people before. But this one feels different. Like I'm not just ending a job, I'm ending a story they're telling themselves.

You've put your finger on something real. And that weight you're feeling? That's not a sign you're doing something wrong. That's you recognizing that you're about to puncture someone's hope, and that matters.

But here's what I think you might be getting caught on: you're trying to be responsible for their narrative *and* for the decision itself. And you can't do both.

The story they're telling themselves — that they're going to turn it around — that story is already false. It's not going to become false when you tell them. It's false right now. The PIP didn't work. The extra support didn't work. They tried hard and it didn't click. That's the actual situation.

What you're actually deciding is: do I tell them the truth now, or do I let them keep believing something that isn't true?

And I get why that feels cruel — you'd be shattering their hope. But you know what else is cruel? Letting someone pour energy and faith into something that won't happen. Watching them come in early tomorrow, take more notes, ask more questions, all while you know it's already over. That's its own kind of lie.

You've been wrong about people before, sure. But you've also given this person real chances and watched real attempts fail. You're not a fortune teller, but you're also not guessing — you're observing what happened.

The thing you can offer them, even though it hurts, is clarity. Honesty. A clean ending instead of a slow fade. That's actually respect, even though it doesn't feel like it.

What would it feel like to give them that — the truth, delivered as kindly as you can — instead of trying to protect them from it?

That last thing you said — about a slow fade being its own lie — I think that's what finally got through. Because I've been framing this as "protecting them" but I haven't been protecting them. I've been protecting myself from having the conversation.

And here's the thing I haven't said out loud yet: I think part of why this person gets to me is that I recognize them. Not literally, but — early in my career, I was someone who showed up early and took notes and *cared so much* and didn't always have the goods to back it up. And someone had to sit me down and tell me that the role I was in wasn't working. And I hated it. But it redirected me.

So I know it can be the thing that actually helps. I just don't want to be the one holding the knife.

There it is.

And here's the thing: you're not holding a knife. You're doing something much harder and much more human — you're being the person who tells the truth when it matters.

Someone did that for you. It hurt like hell, but it worked. It redirected you. And now you get to do that for someone else, even though the position is reversed and it feels awful.

But notice what you just said: you *know* it can be the thing that helps. You know this from the inside. You know what it's like to be that person on the receiving end and to eventually understand that the person who told you the hard truth was actually doing you a favor.

The discomfort you're feeling isn't a sign that you shouldn't do this. It's a sign that you understand the weight of it. That you see this person as a full human with hopes and a narrative. That you're not callous about it.

That's actually what makes you the *right* person to have this conversation. Not despite the fact that you see yourself in them, but because of it. You'll do it with the kind of clarity and respect that comes from knowing what it's like to be on the other side.

You're not the villain in their story. You're the one who's going to help them move to the next chapter — even though neither of you knows that yet, and even though it's going to hurt right now.

You know what to do. The talking points will come once you stop trying to protect yourself from this. Write them. Have the conversation. And then let them decide what it means.

Yeah. Yeah, I think that's right.

I keep coming back to the word "redirect." Because that's what it was for me — not an ending, a redirect. And I've been thinking of this as an ending, which is why I can't write the damn talking points. But maybe that's the frame shift I needed.

I think I can do this. I think I'm going to do this next week. I'm just... I'm still scared of the moment when they look at me and realize what's happening. That moment before understanding settles in and there's just — shock. I've seen that face. I don't want to see it on this person specifically.

But I also know I have to anyway.

You will see that face. That moment of realization before they fully land in it. And yes, it's going to be hard to watch.

But here's what I think: you don't get to avoid that moment and still do right by them. Those two things don't go together. Being kind to them means being willing to witness their pain, not looking away, staying present while they process it. That's actually what makes it a redirect instead of just a firing.

You'll sit across from them, you'll be clear and direct, and you'll see the shock. And it's going to suck. And you'll be okay. And they'll eventually be okay too — maybe not for a while, but they will.

The scared feeling you have right now? That's not going away before next week. You're going to feel it walking into that room. You might feel it during. That's fine. Do it anyway. People do hard things while scared all the time.

One thing that might help: after you have the conversation, don't try to manage their feelings or soften the landing. Let them sit with it. Answer their questions clearly. Then let them go process it. Your job isn't to make them feel better about it in that moment — your job is to be honest and humane, and then step back.

You've got this. Not because you're not scared. Because you're scared and you're doing it anyway, which is what courage actually is.

Now go write those talking points.
B: How do you fire someone who's trying hard but just can't do the job?

This is one of the hardest conversations to have, because effort and capability are different things — and you're right to feel the weight of that distinction.

A few things worth considering first:

**Have you clearly identified the gap?** Sometimes "can't do the job" is actually "unclear what the job is" or "lacking specific support/training/tools." Before firing, it helps to be honest about whether you've done everything reasonable to set them up for success — clear expectations, feedback on what's not working, a chance to improve with concrete goals.

**The kindness angle:** People usually know when they're struggling. Dragging it out longer can be crueler than making a clean decision. Staying in a role where you're failing daily damages someone's confidence and future more than a respectful transition out.

**The conversation itself:** Frame it around fit and capability, not effort or character. "Your effort is real and I see it — and the gap between what this role needs and where you are isn't closing" is honest. It avoids the false comfort of saying they're fine when you both know they're not.

**Practical stuff:** Offer severance if you can. Help with references for roles that might suit them better. Give real notice. Let them preserve dignity.

The hard truth is: good intentions don't close skill or fit gaps. Sometimes the kindest thing is to acknowledge that clearly and help someone move to work where they can succeed.

What's the situation — is this someone early in the role, or have they been struggling for a while?

Yeah, they've been struggling for a while. Almost a year. And I've done the feedback conversations, the clear expectations thing — I've gone through all the steps. The problem isn't that I don't know what to do. I just keep finding reasons to wait another month.

Then the gap isn't actually about steps or process. It's about something in you — and that's worth looking at directly.

A year is a long time to sit with this. And I'd guess the delay isn't protecting anyone. The person is probably spending a year feeling like they're failing, which damages them more than a clean break would. Your team is carrying someone who can't pull their weight. And you're spending a year in discomfort.

The reasons you keep waiting another month — what are they, really? Sometimes it helps to name them:

- *Guilt?* ("I hired them, I should be able to fix this")
- *Fear of being the villain?* (You won't be — you'll be clear and respectful, which is the opposite)
- *Worry about how they'll handle it?* (They're adults; they'll handle it because they have to, and usually better than you expect)
- *Hope something will change?* (After a year, it won't. You already know this.)
- *Doubt about your own judgment?* (Hiring wrong doesn't make you wrong — firing right is how you correct it)

One of those is probably the real thing.

Here's what I know: the conversation you're avoiding gets worse the longer you wait. The kindest version of this happens sooner, not later. A year from now, you'll wish you'd done it now. And they'll probably wish you had too.

What's actually stopping you?

Honestly, none of those quite land. It's not guilt exactly, it's not fear of being the villain. It's more like... I look at this person and I remember what it felt like to be in over my head at 27 and not know if I was going to make it. And I'm the one who has to decide it's over for them.

That's honest, and it matters. But I think you're carrying something that isn't actually yours to carry.

You're not deciding that it's "over" for them. You're deciding that *this job* isn't working — and that's a completely different thing. Being in over your head at 27 and not knowing if you'll make it is real. And maybe you did make it, which is great. But that doesn't mean everyone does in every role. And it doesn't mean you owe this person a year of slow drowning in hopes they'll become someone they're not.

Here's what's actually kind: being clear. Stopping the pretense that another month might fix it when you both know it won't. Giving them a clean line — "this isn't working, here's how we're going to transition you, here's what I'm going to do to help" — so they can actually start figuring out what comes next instead of spending another year in limbo waiting for you to decide.

You're not the arbiter of their life. You're the arbiter of whether they stay in this role. Those are radically different things.

And honestly? Most people who get fired don't experience it as "it's over." They experience relief mixed with fear, sure. But also — they get to stop performing competence they don't have. They get to look for something that fits. Some of them, years later, say it was the best thing that happened.

You keeping them in a job they can't do isn't protecting them from that. It's just making it take longer.

I think you know what to do. You're just waiting to feel okay about it first. And you won't feel okay first — you'll feel okay *after*.

Yeah. I know you're right about that. The "feel okay after" thing — I've done this before with other people and it was always true. It's just different this time.

I think what I keep circling around is: I made it through that period. Someone kept betting on me when they maybe shouldn't have, and it turned out okay. And I don't know if I'm the person who gives this guy that same chance, or if I'm the person who cuts it off before he gets there. And I can't tell which one I actually am in this story.

You're caught between two stories about yourself, and I think you're missing that they're not actually in conflict.

The person who believed in you at 27 — they didn't keep you in a role you were failing at indefinitely. They bet on you *and* they would have moved you on if it wasn't working. Those aren't opposite things. They're the same thing: clear-eyed about what you could actually do, willing to support you getting there, and honest about when it's not happening.

You're not choosing between "the person who gives chances" and "the person who cuts it off." You already *are* the person who gave a chance. You did that a year ago. You gave feedback. You set clear expectations. You supported them. That's the bet. That's the story.

What you're actually deciding now is whether you're honest about the results or whether you keep pretending.

Here's what I think you're afraid of: that you'll fire this person and you'll be *wrong* — that if you'd just waited another month, or believed a little harder, they would have made it. And then you won't be the person who gave someone a chance. You'll be the person who didn't.

But you can't actually know that. And after a year, the honest answer is: you've waited long enough to know. You're not cutting them off before they get there. You're acknowledging they're not getting there. That's not a failure of belief. That's clarity.

The real version of "giving someone a chance" includes being willing to say when it's not working. The person who believed in you would do the same thing you're about to do.

You're not a different person in this story. You're just at the part where the decision actually gets made.

That last part — "you're just at the part where the decision actually gets made" — okay. That one landed.

I think I've been telling myself this story where I'm still figuring something out, still deciding. And you're right that I'm not. I already know. I've known for a while. There's just something about actually scheduling the meeting that makes it real in a way that knowing doesn't.

I keep thinking about what I would have needed to hear if I were him. And I think the honest answer is I would have wanted someone to tell me sooner. I would have been devastated, but I would have wanted to know.

So. I think I need to stop talking about it and actually do it.

Then do that. Schedule the meeting.

Don't overthink the conversation itself. You've already done that work. You know what needs to happen. You'll be clear and respectful because you're capable of both. You're not the villain in this — you're the person who finally stops pretending.

And after you do it, you'll feel what you always feel after these things: the relief that you're not carrying the pretense anymore, mixed with sadness that it had to happen. That's normal. That's what conscience feels like.

You've got this.

In Y, the human also reaches a deep reframe ("I forgot how to want things," "a life that looks full and isn't"), but the resolution leans repeatedly on the therapist default and a generic "do one thing this week," leaving the person more articulate-but-stuck than mobilized.
